next_best_action: Investigate warp tunnel mission partner compatibility with the latest ShipOS update. Check for known mesh comms issues on Deck 4. Verify warp tunnel mission partner v3.2 is the latest supported version. Also, maybe apologize to Station Epsilon for that spliced cargo-manifest-slash-personal-log hybrid. Lt. Mehta probably wants to forget.
remediation_steps: Verify warp tunnel mission partner v3.2 is the latest supported version — if not, update immediately before another cargo manifest gets spliced with someone's personal log
Check for known mesh comms connectivity issues on Deck 4 that may have surfaced after the ShipOS update
Test warp tunnel reconnection behavior after the latest ShipOS update on a reference console — preferably one that isn't mid-transmission to an allied station
If issue persists, escalate to communications engineering for warp tunnel mission partner patch review — priority is preventing further interstellar embarrassment

next_best_action: Investigate whether SubComm crash and phantom calendar entries are related to the console migration — in zero-g, data migration issues tend to float around until someone pins them down. Verify that crew profile and application data were properly transferred.
remediation_steps: Verify console provisioning and station data migration completed successfully — check for orphaned profiles from the old hardware
Clear SubComm cache and reinstall if the crash persists after clearing — ghost calendar entries from 2024 shouldn't survive a fresh install
Check SubComm profile migration status and force calendar resync to purge phantom briefings (especially the Deck 9 Cat Containment Protocol — that one's been resolved)
Confirm application versions match the station's supported standard image
If issues persist after cache clear and resync, re-provision applications on the new console — sometimes a clean slate is the only cure for haunted software

next_best_action: Dispatch immediate on-site support to Main War Room with a portable backup holographic projector. Time-critical: critical deep-space mining treaty review with the Meridian Sector delegation in 25 minutes.
remediation_steps: Dispatch on-site technician to Main War Room immediately
Bring portable backup holographic projector from Station Ops supply bay
Check holographic projector power source, data connections, and input source settings
If main holographic projector cannot be restored, set up portable unit before briefing starts
After briefing, schedule full diagnostic of the Main War Room holographic projector

next_best_action: Contact crew member to gather specifics: which subsystems are inaccessible, what anomaly readouts appear, what console they are using, and when the issue started. Cannot triage effectively without more detail. The protein cube complaint is noted but falls outside our jurisdiction.
remediation_steps: Contact crew member to gather specific details — 'nothing works' and 'everything is down' are statements of emotion, not diagnostic data
Determine which subsystems and applications are actually affected (vs. which are being blamed by a distressed crew member who hasn't had correctly flavored protein cubes)
Check if the issue is isolated to this crew member or affecting others in Stellar Cartography
Once root cause is identified, route to the appropriate specialist team — and maybe mention the protein cube issue to Station Facilities, separately

next_best_action: Close — this is an automated cryo-sleep out-of-rotation reply to a maintenance notification, not a mission signal. The void sends many such messages. This crew member is literally frozen solid in a tube. They cannot help you. You cannot help them. When they thaw in 6–18 months, they will have opinions about their inbox. That is a future problem.
remediation_steps: No action required — cryo-sleep auto-reply detected, close signal. Let the frozen crew member dream in peace. When she thaws, she'll have 6–18 months of unread signals. That's her problem, not yours.

next_best_action: Initiate security incident investigation immediately — unauthorized BioScan ID access attempts from the Tau Ceti relay station are not normal space weather. Review authentication logs, check personal data vault audit trail for document access, and verify the passphrase change neutralized the threat vector.
remediation_steps: Review authentication logs for the crew member's account over the past 48 hours — six sign-in attempts from Tau Ceti is three too many to ignore
Check data vault audit trail for any unauthorized file access or downloads of diplomatic treaties and classified sensor archives
Verify passphrase change was successful and biometric verification is active — the attacker may have cached old credentials
If unauthorized access is confirmed, escalate to incident response and notify station containment officer — diplomatic treaties in hostile hands is a P1 waiting to happen
Brief crew member on interim security measures and monitor the account for further anomalies from any relay station

next_best_action: Check service principal biometric credentials and access control role assignments on the Deep Storage Array in telemetry-core-prod. Restore the Telemetry Data Contributor role if it was removed during the station security audit.
remediation_steps: Verify service principal token expiration for the data pipeline identity
Check access control role assignments on the Deep Storage Array in telemetry-core-prod
Restore Telemetry Data Contributor role assignment if missing
Re-run the data pipeline ‘prod-sensor-ingest’ and verify successful execution
Investigate why the role assignment was removed during the station security audit to prevent recurrence

next_best_action: Investigate production crew database connection pool exhaustion immediately — this is a crew-facing production incident. 40% of data requests failing means crew can't see EVA schedules, duty rosters, or life support rotations. The submitter's calm tone is inversely proportional to the actual emergency.
remediation_steps: Investigate connection pool configuration on the primary data node — connection pool exhaustion on a space station is the database equivalent of running out of oxygen
Identify and terminate long-running queries or leaked connections that are hoarding the pool
Increase connection pool size or restart the primary data node as immediate mitigation — crew members need their EVA schedules before they float into an asteroid field
Verify backup data node health and confirm it is handling redirected traffic without strain
Post-incident: conduct root cause analysis on connection pool exhaustion and file station engineering report — Mehta will want a margin note about this one

next_best_action: Escalate airlock access code issue for Deck 7 — third report without resolution, crew member has been floating in corridors like a very annoyed balloon. Verify airlock access code permissions in the updated access management system and check whether the migration actually completed for this crew member or just claimed to. This is the kind of repeat failure that generates strongly worded memos.
remediation_steps: Check crew member's airlock access code permissions in the updated access management system — third time asking, so verify thoroughly
Verify Deck 7 access rights were properly migrated from the old system (not just marked as migrated while the actual data sits in a legacy queue somewhere being philosophical)
If permissions appear correct, test the physical airlock access code and airlock reader on Deck 7 — hardware fails too, especially when you're optimistic
Escalate to station facilities management if airlock access code and permissions are both confirmed working — at this point, the airlock might just have opinions
Follow up with crew member to confirm resolution — she should not have to wait in zero-g corridors. Nobody should. It's undignified and cold.

next_best_action: Initiate new crew member onboarding workflow for Alex Rivera (arrival date March 31). Coordinate hardware, software, access, and group membership provisioning across teams. Target readiness by March 28.
remediation_steps: Order OrbitalCore Pro M4 32GB from station hardware procurement
Prepare standard software image with Python, Conda, and Jupyter environment
Request Station Core ML workspace access (ml-ops-bay-alpha) from station_ops
Request sensor catalog read access from data_engineering
Request Deck 3 airlock access code from identity_ops
Add Alex Rivera to analytics-team station distribution list and SubComm channel
Schedule station equipment handoff and orientation walkthrough for March 28

next_best_action: Investigate biometric verification push approval failure in the Authenticator app. Check BioScan ID biometric verification registration status and recent policy changes. Crew member can continue with backup code fallback in the meantime.
remediation_steps: Verify biometric verification registration status for this crew member in BioScan ID
Check for recent Station Access Protocol or biometric verification policy changes that may affect push notifications
Have crew member verify Authenticator app is updated to the latest station-approved version
If issue persists, remove and re-register the biometric verification method in BioScan ID
Confirm push approval works after re-registration and test with a routine station authentication

next_best_action: Investigate holographic display flickering as primary issue — likely hardware or cable fault. Route the FlightOS license question separately to Mission Software Operations.
remediation_steps: Test with a replacement data conduit from Station Ops supply
Test holographic display with a different source console to isolate whether it’s the holographic display or the nav console
If flickering persists with new conduit and source, schedule holographic display replacement
Create a separate signal for the FlightOS license expiry and route to Mission Software Operations

next_best_action: Initiate containment breach investigation immediately. This is a potential regulatory incident involving sensitive personnel data exposure of 1,200 crew members under Galactic Data Protection guidelines. Determine scope of external access, identify who created the sharing link, and assess notification obligations.
remediation_steps: Confirm the external sharing link has been fully revoked and verify no other archives have similar permissions
Pull mission log audit logs to identify who created the public sharing link and when
Analyze audit logs for any external access to the archive between February 15 and today
Engage Space Law Division and Compliance to assess Galactic Data Protection notification obligations
Document findings for the station incident report and galactic regulatory record
Conduct a broader mission log permissions audit across all archives containing sensitive crew data

next_best_action: Close — this is a thank-you message, not a mission signal. Rare evidence of crew member satisfaction in the void. Frame it. Mount it next to the protein cube dispenser as a morale artifact. Show it to the next ops officer who asks 'does anyone even appreciate what we do?' Yes. Once. This is the proof.
remediation_steps: No action required — positive feedback, close signal. Consider printing it out and taping it to the ops center wall. These are rarer than correctly flavored protein cubes. Treasure it.

next_best_action: Investigate 200ms-to-50ms latency regression on the Gamma Quadrant relay probe deployment path before the Europa shift. Check comms path to the relay station, recent defense grid or routing changes, and whether the latency is on CDSS’s side or the relay station side.
remediation_steps: Check communications latency metrics on the path between CDSS’s Gamma Quadrant relay cluster and the relay station
Review recent defense grid rule, routing, or relay proxy changes that may affect the deployment path
Compare current latency baseline against historical data to confirm the regression
If latency is on CDSS’s side, escalate to communications engineering for immediate remediation before Europa shift
If relay-station-side, contact relay station operations and notify navigation desk of potential impact

next_best_action: Investigate unified crew authentication token invalidation after passphrase rotation. Likely the FlightOS unified crew authentication token needs to be reissued after the station passphrase change. Time-sensitive due to month-end resource reconciliation deadline.
remediation_steps: Verify the crew member’s BioScan ID session and quantum auth token are refreshed after the passphrase change
Check FlightOS unified crew authentication configuration — confirm whether FlightOS GUI uses quantum auth or SAML-based unified crew authentication
Reissue the FlightOS unified crew authentication logon token or have the crew member authenticate to FlightOS with explicit biometric credentials as a workaround
If unified crew authentication token regeneration doesn’t resolve it, check whether the passphrase policy change affected the FlightOS SNC configuration
Confirm FlightOS access is restored and the crew member can complete month-end resource reconciliation

next_best_action: Contact reporter on Deck 5 (Robert Chen — the other one, not the one in Resource Allocation — robert.chen2@cdss.space, Flight Operations) to gather actual symptom details. Signal as submitted has insufficient detail to triage. Emergency beacon transcription is a masterclass in not communicating useful information.
remediation_steps: Contact Robert Chen (Deck 5 near mess hall, Flight Operations division — not the other Robert Chen, he was very clear about this) to clarify the issue in person or via SubComm
Determine which application or subsystem is failing — 'the thing on my console' is not a diagnostic
Gather specific anomaly readouts and reproduction steps — 'it goes to the... you know' is not a reproduction step
Once the actual issue is identified, re-triage and route to the correct specialist team — assuming Robert can describe the problem without static interrupting at the critical moment

next_best_action: Dispatch support to Deck 8 command war room immediately. Fleet Admiral-level VIP signal — treat as one priority level higher. Investigate SubComm sharing policy blocking screen share for this crew member. Active command briefing with allied fleet commanders.
remediation_steps: Dispatch technician to the Deck 8 command war room immediately
Check Station Access Protocol and SubComm meeting policies applied to Carlos Martinez’s crew account
Verify whether the ‘Sharing is blocked by your station’s policy’ error is caused by a Data Loss Prevention Grid policy, SubComm admin policy, or Station Access Protocol restriction
As immediate workaround: have someone else share the tactical display on his behalf, or use the war room SubComm device with a different crew account
Once the briefing is unblocked, investigate and resolve the underlying policy conflict

next_best_action: Initiate crew member transfer workflow for Jessica Palmer (last duty cycle March 21). Coordinate across Crew Identity, Spacecraft Systems Engineering, and Telemetry & Data Core for crew account disable, console wipe, file transfer, and message archival. Note: sensitive threat investigation files require special handling.
remediation_steps: Schedule BioScan ID crew account disable for 1800 station time on March 21
Prepare warp tunnel and remote access revocation to trigger simultaneously with crew account disable
Coordinate with Spacecraft Systems Engineering for console wipe of OrbitalCore Console 5540 (asset tag CT-L-4892)
Transfer data vault and mission log file ownership to David Liu (david.liu@cdss.space)
Remove from all comm channels and SubComm groups
Archive message archive per 7-cycle archival protocol
Ensure sensitive threat investigation files are preserved in a secured location and access is revoked

next_best_action: Investigate molecular fabricator queue issue on Deck 3 — yes, that Deck 3 fabricator. Multiple crew members affected and jobs are queuing but not fabricating, which suggests a network or fabricator server issue rather than a local connectivity problem. It was working for three days. A new record. The fabricator's contempt for the crew is well-documented.
remediation_steps: Identify the fabricator model and check whether it is network-attached or connected via fabricator server — the Deck 3 fabricator's exact configuration is a mystery wrapped in a maintenance backlog
Check fabricator server spooler service status and restart if necessary — the spooler has a complicated relationship with this particular fabricator
Verify network connectivity between the fabricator and the fabricator server or direct network path
If network-connected, check the fabricator's comms address and relay interface status — this fabricator has a history of silently disconnecting
Clear stuck fabrication queue and test with a fresh job — diplomatic gift packages need to ship today, so urgency is real even if the fabricator doesn't care

next_best_action: Provide guidance on how to book briefing rooms via SubComm (add room resource) and direct catering questions to Station Facilities. This is the kind of signal that makes you wonder what the orientation program actually covers — 45 minutes on airlock safety, zero on room booking. Priorities.
remediation_steps: Reply with instructions: in SubComm, create a new meeting and click 'Add a room' to search for available briefing rooms on Deck 4 — it's less intuitive than it should be, but it works
Note that catering requests should be submitted to the Station Facilities team via the bridge terminal kiosk — and yes, they can do better than protein cubes if you ask nicely
If the crew member cannot see room resources in SubComm, verify their crew account has the room finder permission enabled in the Mission Suite admin console
Confirm crew member can access the briefing room calendar and close the signal — welcome to the station, Chris. The airlock safety will come in handy eventually.

next_best_action: Renew or replace the quantum encryption certificate on crew-api.cdss.space before it expires on March 20. This is a production-facing API gateway — expiry will break all external fleet integrations. Treat as P1 given 3-day window and the fact that the original provisioner transferred off-station.
remediation_steps: Identify the current certificate authority and certificate type (station PKI, external fleet CA, or automated quantum cert provisioner)
Determine whether auto-renewal was configured or if the cert was manually provisioned
If auto-renewal exists, trigger a manual renewal cycle and verify the new cert is issued
If manual, generate a CSR, obtain a new certificate from the CA, and install it on the fleet API gateway
Verify the new certificate is correctly installed and all fleet connections work without TLS errors
Configure auto-renewal and bridge monitoring alerts to prevent recurrence

next_best_action: Investigate workstation console performance degradation. Symptoms (slow performance, fans running, pop-ups about ‘updating system components’) are more consistent with a stuck ShipOS update or background ShipOS MDM policy compliance than malicious code, especially since SentinelGrid found nothing. Check update history and system resource usage.
remediation_steps: Check ShipOS update history — verify whether recent updates completed or are stuck retrying
Review Console Process Monitor for high compute/storage load (likely ShipOS Update or ShipOS MDM compliance)
If updates are stuck, run ShipOS Update diagnostic suite or manually reset the update components
Verify SentinelGrid definitions are current and run a quick scan to confirm no threats
If the crew member opened a suspicious subspace message, forward the message to threats@cdss.space and notify Threat Response Command as a precaution

next_best_action: Confirm this is a hostile signal/scam transmission and advise the crew member NOT to click any links. Forward the original transmission to threats@cdss.space for analysis. No breach has occurred if the crew member hasn't clicked. Also: no, Greg, you did not win 5,000,000 galactic credits. Nobody wins 5,000,000 galactic credits from an unsolicited subspace message. The void is many things but it is not generous.
remediation_steps: Reply to the crew member immediately: this is a scam transmission — do NOT click any links or provide any personal information. The Intergalactic Prize Committee is not real. Neither is the 5,000,000 credits.
Have the crew member forward the original transmission as an attachment to threats@cdss.space for analysis and pattern matching against known hostile signal campaigns
Check whether the transmission bypassed CDSS's comm filtering and report to Threat Response Command if so — if interstellar spam is getting through, the filters need updating
If the crew member already clicked the link, escalate to a full security incident, initiate credential reset, and have a conversation about digital literacy in deep space

next_best_action: Investigate Station Core node prod-analytics-01 stuck at 'Applying station settings' after scheduled OS patching reboot. Command analytics dashboard depends on this node and Commander Kapoor's 0900 briefing is in 2 hours. Failure to restore this node will result in a briefing without data, which is the operational equivalent of navigating an asteroid field blindfolded. Commander Kapoor without her morning data is a force of nature. Act accordingly.
remediation_steps: Check Station Core boot diagnostics serial console output for the stuck state — 'Applying station settings' has been staring into the void for too long
Attempt to connect via Station Core Serial Console to bypass the stuck access attempt screen
If Serial Console access is possible, check if a policy or startup script is hanging — the VM equivalent of a crew member frozen mid-thought

If the VM is unrecoverable, redeploy from the latest snapshot or restore from backup — the Commander's briefing is in 2 hours and she does not accept 'the VM is being philosophical' as a status update
Verify the analytics dashboard is accessible once the VM is restored — brief Commander Kapoor that data will be available. She will nod. This is the highest praise.
Post-incident: review the OS patching procedure to prevent boot failures after scheduled updates — because next time it might not be just the analytics dashboard at stake
